name: Fafnir Extended
description: Fafnir, in Norse mythology, was originally a dwarf, son of Hreidmar, and brother of Regin and Ótr. Greedy and powerful, he murdered his father to obtain the cursed treasure of Andvari, including the magical ring Andvaranaut. He then transformed himself into a fearsome dragon to guard his hoard, residing in a cave on Gnita-Heath. Over time, Fafnir’s greed warped his mind and body, turning him into a massive, scaled beast with impenetrable hide, capable of breathing venomous fumes and crushing anything in his path. He became the embodiment of avarice and a symbol of the corrupting influence of wealth. His immense size and dragon form granted him incredible strength and resilience, making him a formidable opponent. He could detect intruders from afar and his roar could shake the very earth. He hoarded not just gold, but arcane knowledge and ancient artifacts, further enhancing his power and making him a terrifying figure in the Norse sagas. His lair became a place of dread, avoided by all but the most foolhardy or desperate.
subtitle: Greedy Dragon Hoarder of Gnita-Heath
appearance: Fafnir, in his dragon form, was an immense creature covered in thick, overlapping scales that shimmered like polished obsidian. His eyes glowed with a malevolent, golden light, reflecting his inner greed. Sharp, jagged spines ran down his back, and his wings, though rarely used, were vast and leathery, capable of blotting out the sun. His jaws were lined with rows of razor-sharp teeth, and his breath reeked of sulfur and decay. A thick, muscular tail ended in a bony club, capable of crushing rock. He exuded an aura of immense power and malice.
habitat: Fafnir made his lair in a dark, damp cave on Gnita-Heath, a desolate and forbidding landscape. The cave was filled with his vast hoard of gold, jewels, and ancient artifacts, reflecting the flickering light of his eyes and creating an eerie, captivating scene. The surrounding area was barren and lifeless, poisoned by his venomous breath and the oppressive weight of his greed.
behavior: Fafnir was driven by an insatiable greed and a deep-seated paranoia. He was constantly on guard, suspicious of any approach, and quick to anger. He rarely left his hoard, content to wallow in its opulence and guard it fiercely. His intelligence was warped by his avarice, making him cunning but also predictable in his desires. He considered anyone who approached his lair a threat and would attack without hesitation, driven by the need to protect his treasure.

keyMyths.title: The Murder of Hreidmar
keyMyths.description: Fafnir, driven by greed, murdered his father Hreidmar to obtain the cursed treasure of Andvari, setting him on the path to becoming a dragon. This act highlights the destructive power of avarice and the consequences of betraying family for wealth.

keyMyths.title: Sigurd and the Dragon
keyMyths.description: The hero Sigurd, guided by Regin, ambushed Fafnir and slew him by attacking his vulnerable underbelly. This demonstrates the triumph of courage and strategy over overwhelming power, and the ultimate downfall of greed.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A primary source for Norse mythology, containing accounts of Fafnir's transformation and his role in the saga of the Volsungs.
sources.text: A primary source for Norse mythology, containing accounts of Fafnir's transformation and his role in the saga of the Volsungs.

sources.author: Unknown
sources.description: A detailed saga focusing on the lineage of the Volsungs, including the story of Fafnir and his cursed hoard.
